ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for soy-braised egg. Closest parent 'hen egg food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (hen eggs, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for soy-braised egg exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 (Eggs, edible) is the closest parent concept. No specific concept for braised egg exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One braised egg weighing approximately 55 g, typically served as a side dish or accompaniment to Bak Kut Teh or rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129); adapted with soy-braising adjustment for sodium and minor carbohydrate uptake from marinade
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 173424). Sodium adjusted upward to approximately 350 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce marinade absorption, consistent with published values for soy-marinated eggs in peer-reviewed Southeast Asian food composition literature. Carbohydrate adjusted to approximately 2.5 g per 100 g to reflect sugar and soy sauce uptake during braising. All other macros retained from USDA hard-boiled egg baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; eggs and egg-based foods GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for soy-braised egg (Lu Dan). GI estimated at 13 based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference for hard-boiled egg (GI ~13, low category). Soy braising adds minimal carbohydrate (~2.5 g per 100 g) and does not materially alter the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 55 g serving = (13 × 2.5 × 0.55) / 100 ≈ 0.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce marinade (~350 mg per 100 g; ~193 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit braised egg consumption to one egg per meal and avoid consuming the braising liquid, as the soy sauce marinade contributes significant sodium that can elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~13) and minimal carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised egg is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load per serving; however, the high cholesterol content (~373 mg per 100 g) warrants moderation in those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (~373 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~3.3 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit braised egg intake to no more than one egg per day, as the high cholesterol and saturated fat content may contribute to adverse lipid profiles when consumed in excess; the elevated sodium from soy braising also poses an additional cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised egg (卤蛋, Lu Dan) is a popular Southeast Asian and Chinese dish consisting of a hard-boiled hen egg that has been slow-cooked and marinated in a savory braising liquid typically made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, five-spice powder, garlic, and water. The extended braising process imparts a deep caramelized brown color to the egg white, a rich umami-soy flavor, and a slightly firm yet tender texture throughout. In Singapore and Malaysia, braised eggs are commonly served as a side dish accompanying Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup), rice dishes, or noodle soups, and are also sold as standalone street food snacks. Nutritionally, braised eggs are a high-protein, moderate-fat food with very low carbohydrate content. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 155 kcal, 12.6 g protein, 10.6 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate. The soy sauce marinade significantly elevates sodium content to approximately 350 mg per 100 g, which is a key consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (~13), making braised eggs suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Cholesterol content is high (~373 mg per 100 g), consistent with egg-based foods, and should be considered in the context of overall dietary cholesterol intake. Braised eggs are a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin A, phosphorus, and selenium.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a braised egg?
Is braised egg good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in a braised egg?
What is the GI of braised egg?
Can I eat braised egg if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between braised egg and regular boiled egg?
What does braised egg taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for soy-braised egg (Lu Dan) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg baseline (FDC ID 173424) with defensible adjustments for sodium and carbohydrate uptake from soy braising. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database hard-boiled egg reference. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424). Sodium has been adjusted upward to ~350 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce marinade absorption during braising; this is consistent with published ranges for soy-marinated eggs in Southeast Asian food composition literature. Carbohydrate adjusted to ~2.5 g per 100 g to reflect sugar and soy sauce uptake. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in hen eggs per USDA data. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as eggs contain no dietary fibre per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as eggs contain no vitamin C per USDA SR Legacy. 3. GI estimated at 13 based on University of Sydney GI Database hard-boiled egg reference; no direct GI measurement for soy-braised egg exists in the approved GI database. 4. FoodOn match type is closest_parent (FOODON_03301710, hen egg food product); no exact FoodOn term for soy-braised egg exists. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent (A00LC, hen eggs cooked); no exact FoodEx2 code for soy-braised egg exists. SNOMED CT match type is closest_parent (102263004, Eggs edible). 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424) and are not set to -1 as they are reported in the source. 6. The food is tagged as Malaysian cuisine per the input record and is served in Singapore at Legendary Bak Kut Teh; multilingual names cover English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Singaporean/Malaysian dishes. 7. Halal status is true as eggs are halal; gluten_free is false as soy sauce typically contains wheat.
